tests.source_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-p:32:32-i64:64"

define i32 @smul_i16_operands(i16 %a, i16 %b) {
  %conv = sext i16 %a to i32
  %conv1 = sext i16 %b to i32
  %mul = mul nsw i32 %conv1, %conv
  %1 = add nsw i32 %mul, -32768
  %2 = icmp ult i32 %1, -65536
  %conv3 = zext i1 %2 to i32
  ret i32 %conv3
}
tests.expect_optimized_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-p:32:32-i64:64"

define i32 @smul_i16_operands(i16 %a, i16 %b) {
  %ext.a = sext i16 %a to i32
  %ext.b = sext i16 %b to i32
  %smul = call { i32, i1 } @llvm.smul.with.overflow.i32(i32 %ext.a, i32 %ext.b)
  %overflow = extractvalue { i32, i1 } %smul, 1
  %conv3 = zext i1 %overflow to i32
  ret i32 %conv3
}

declare { i32, i1 } @llvm.smul.with.overflow.i32(i32, i32)
tests.outside_scope_reason: Summary focuses on i32/i64 examples but doesn't explicitly mention i16 operand types. The code handles any integer types by computing common multiplication width, but this specific smaller type combination isn't detailed in the summary.

tests.source_program:
target datalayout = "e-m:e-p:32:32-i64:64"

define i32 @smul_branch_condition(i32 %a, i32 %b) {
  %conv = sext i32 %a to i64
  %conv1 = sext i32 %b to i64
  %mul = mul nsw i64 %conv1, %conv
  %1 = add nsw i64 %mul, -2147483648
  %2 = icmp ult i64 %1, -4294967296
  br i1 %2, label %overflow, label %no_overflow
overflow:
  ret i32 -1
no_overflow:
  ret i32 0
}
tests.expect_optimized_program:

  br i1 %overflow, label %overflow, label %no_overflow

no_overflow:
